sentences.text: Yes.
You see, and my father hung up the phone.
He, you know-- the point about this is this became known around our community.
What I think-- you see, even I had some difficulty realizing at the time.
Though of course, I wasn't no hero like my father was.
His response to this man, who was a stormtrooping leader of some sort, could have cost him his life.
I mean, people were killed for less.
Well, I would not say a lot, no.
There was one occasion where the rumor was brought to me that they were going to wait for me and beat me up.
I waited for quite a while and then told my parents, who apparently made a phone call to the school.
And this was squashed.
This didn't happen.
The other kind of anti-Semitism was the teachers.
I mean, really we all felt sorry for them.
I remember one [NON-ENGLISH] who tried to teach us history.
Well, I enjoyed history.
I paid attention.
Besides, I was Jewish.
I was going to do well.
So whenever he asked, you know, what he'd done the last time, my hand would go up.
And sooner or later, he'd have to call on me.
And I would give him-- well, every so often, he would talk about the Jews failing to fight in the-- the World War and [? and they're ?] cowards, [? they were here, ?] et cetera, et cetera.
He had to do it.
I mean, right?
Well, he was with my father in the war, see?
So he knew he was talking rubbish.
I knew he was talking rubbish.
And he knew I knew.
But up went my hand.
And I would return to him exactly the sort of thing he had said.
I was told later that while I was absent at one time, he-- so would always beg the class.
He couldn't-- you know, couldn't be the Jew were the only one who knew the history.
Would they please do something?
Well, I don't know if this is terribly anti-Semitic, but it's, I suppose, indicative of what's going on.
You see?
Mhm.
As I say, in the school, the-- the faculty and the administration, if anything, protected us.
They tried to be as fair and as decent-- it wasn't for a long while we were there, you know?
Until at the end of-- toward the end of '38, before Kristallnacht, the headmaster, the director-- the [NON-ENGLISH] director called us to his office.
And with clearly embarrassment and regret, told us that we couldn't attend any longer, that there was a law out now that-- to-- to stop us.
And I went to-- well, my parents sent me to Berlin, to a Jewish school.
Not quite as humanistic as the Humanistic Gymnasium, but it's still along those lines.
Now, this man, I'm quite certain, was not meant to [? seem ?] [? mad. ?] And I think he was sorry to have to do this.
On the other hand, he also didn't resign or something, or say-- or say, to heck with you.
Very, very few did that.

When-- you know, you've heard this said, I think, that until the final solution got into action-- you know, the-- the thing that Spielberg wrote about-- showed the movie about, and the-- the other movie about the Shoah and so on.
Until that happened, was a very salami activity, a piece of-- slice of salami.
One thing happened.
Then another thing happened.
Then another thing happened, and so on.
I can show you, I think, the documents that they sent my father, to first stop his being a notary.
In Germany, when you were a lawyer for a certain time and had a certain reputation, you were given the Office of Notary, which was a bit more than what we do here.
But that was taken away.
Then he was not permitted to represent any Aryans, any Germans, only Jews.
And then he was-- that was taken away.
And he was-- he had no more-- no more livelihood.
While he was defending Jews, the story I get is that you know, some Jew had been caught trying to smuggle some money across a border on which the Nazis had put the death penalty.
And he was in big trouble.
And you know, would this lawyer defend him?
He said, no, I couldn't possibly.
He's got a wife and children.
He can't to take the chance.
But he says, why don't you go see Hartmann?
He may be crazy enough to take your case.
And Hartmann, invariably, was.
And had some successes.
There's a story that in one-- in one case, during the-- during the break in the case, he turned to the journalists.
And said well, gentlemen, of course we are quite-- we quite understand that if my client were not a Jew, he would have been released, freed, long ago.
And when the judge came back, the man was freed within minutes.
Another time, the prosecutor made a passionate speech about these dastardly Jews doing this, that, and the other.
You know-- you know, it was this terrible crook.
And as a sharpening condition-- what's the term-- not extenuating circumstances.
What is a circumstance that makes it worse?
Extenuating?
No, extenuating makes it better.
But as a particularly nasty aspect of this whole this is--
This dirty so-and-so is even a Jew.
And my father, in his calm response, pointed to the laws.
Said, the fact that my client is a Jew is not making it worse.
That's an extenuating circumstance.
They want to live too.
And this kind of thing did get a few people either off or very, very mildly punished.
But he, you know, he defended-- OK, do you know what [INAUDIBLE] was?
You know what the stormtroopers were.
Hitler's troops.
Well, there were other troops around.
The [INAUDIBLE] were the, more or less, the Social Democratic Party.
Had the movement, practicing, of-- you know, they're at battle.
There are street battles.
And my father was a member of the [INAUDIBLE].
I don't think he was a socialist.
But he felt that this was necessary.
And I know that in '33, my father expected to be called out.
There would have been civil war.
But the question of Hitler's [? sweet ?] victory was fairly-- it was not clear.
But they were not called out.
And my mother then, one night, took his uniform, jacket, and his bayonet, and took it out into the field and buried it, and you know.
But my father defended [INAUDIBLE] from the accusation of having murdered Nazis before what is the equivalent of the Supreme Court in Germany, in the city of [INAUDIBLE].
And he-- again, they were not killed.
There were some-- you know, he had some success in-- in turning away the worst aspect of a scene.
So you see, he was quite known.
And he was respected by the courts and by the police.
But obviously, he wasn't very popular with the, you know, the stormtroopers, with the Nazi bunch.
On November the 10th, 9th, the Kristallnacht--
Anyway, that was--
--you know, his lawyer and his--
OK.
Yes.
Well, I remember that he was perfectly straightforwardly elected.
And we tend to forget that.
That he had a proper majority.
And I mean, I know that I didn't like it.
I mean, this was-- this was-- it was not good for the Jews.
More seriously, that this was not something that we would have wished.
But as I told you before, I was 11 at the time.
And our parents went out of their way to try to protect us.
So that I don't know that this made a very major-- had a very major influence on my life.
Except on little things like, you know, my mother, very soon after he was in power, they took-- got out the Nuremberg laws and so on.
And I remember my mother very sharply saying to my brother and me, to whatever we did, never to look at, never mind talk to or touch, a German Aryan girl.
Well, then a little bit later, you see, '34, '35-- so it went step by step.
Jews were prohibited to go to the parks.
They were prohibited to go to the movies.
I'm not quite sure what the sequence of this was.
One after the other.
And finally, long before Kristallnacht, we are not permitted to go to museums or theaters and so on, and so on.

And I remember my mother taking me to the German-- to the Chemnitz theater-- which was quite a respectable theater-- when a famous German actress by the name of Kathe Dorsch-- I remember her-- was there to play Schiller, a game-- a play by Schiller.
And my mother took us.
Had she been recognized, have somebody said anything, God knows what would have happened.
But she and I got away with it.
Again, another time, she took us, took me, travelled with me to Dresden, which was fairly near to Chemnitz.
And Dresden, as you may recall, has a very fine art, you know, places.
Among them, the famous Zwinger Museum, where she showed me some pictures.
I remember seeing the Sistine Madonna, by Raphael.
And I must say, I'm grateful to her for this, to this day.
Because I haven't forgotten it.
That was, to me, I think, one of the first major experiences of what real art can be and can do.
But it was illegal.
You see, that was illegal.
And my mother risked something doing this.
Well, I mean, Hitler made no secrecy of his dislike for Jews.
I mean, that was--
You know, I can't answer that.
I simply don't remember.
But there was so much in the air, so much in the-- in the-- well, newspapers, radio, all kinds of things, that it was really rather difficult to avoid this.
How I specifically came to this particular conclusion, I don't know.
I'm not sure there was a very definite conclusion.
It was a feeling we had.
I guess.
Yes, I was.
I became bar mitzvahed.
No, a little later.
No, no.
You see, my parents were rather good, my mother especially, I think, at keeping all this away from the house and us.
We knew, generally, about it.
But this didn't play an immediate part in our young lives.
I'm not sure whether I'm saying this well.
But--
Well, I mean, that nonsense about Aryan girls and Jewish, with that, what I told you before, that my mother said don't you, you know, look at or talk to or let alone, touch, any girl.
I suppose that affects me.
The-- I forget it was the Nuremberg laws, I mean, yes, I have a feeling it was a Nuremberg law that Jews were told they were no longer [GERMAN], that is free, recognized citizens.
But they are sort of-- something, it's a slightly different word.
I've forgotten right now what it was.
Volks-- ?] no, it was the other way around.
They were not [?
Volks ?] [GERMAN], that is, belonging to the people.
They were simply and only citizens of the state, that kind of thing.
Again, I can't think of anything specific that this-- that changed in my life.
You see, what it obviously did was, it made it less and less easy or possible to deal-- to deal with, to talk to non-Jewish youngsters.
I remember one very bright youngster asked me to come to his house to talk to him about the Jews, because what he had heard.
So he wanted to check out.
He wanted to check on it.
That boy was unusual.
He was very brave.
Or his parents were, or both were.
I did go to his house.
And we had an hour's conversation or something like that.
And he thanked me.
And, you know, that was it.
So there's that, I suppose.
You see, the social life was [? affected. ?] Then of course, we never knew how long we could stay in the German school.
And this business I was talking to you about before, my hitting that boy, or they're planning to beat me up, or this business of being looked down upon, you see-- I mean, it was part of daily life.
You see?
And it became more and more difficult as time went on.
That is the famous salami tactics, you see.
Tell me about when you were at home.
Mhm.
Not with us.
Not with us, not with the children.
No.
Well, the question-- many people did leave, and so on and so on.
And I knew quite early my father did not wish to leave.
I recall a little later, asking him.
I said, look, you always thought yourself-- you said you are the German.
You're a German patriot.
You fought for Germany.
And I remember what he said.
He said, my position in life and my attitudes toward it are not determined by some person like Adolf Hitler.
In other words, he never gave up.
Yes.
Yes.
I mean, there were stories about so-and-so having left, and such-and-such about to leave.
We never made any move in that direction until Kristallnacht.
When-- when you were-- after-- after 1933, did you have a best friend that was Jewish?
Well, I must have had.
There was my cousin in Berlin.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
Well, he was the son of my mother's sister, and of course, her husband.
He was a year older than I.
And he was a very nice guy.
No.
No.
It just wasn't talked about.
The only, quote, 'political,' unquote, discussion I remember was before Hitler came to power, while I was still in elementary school.
And some kid asked me what I was, as far as the parties are concerned.
And I told him you know, I was a Social Democrat or something like that.
But my parents were not.
And so-- but that was child stuff and of no-- of no real meaning.
But that's the only political discussion that I recall.
No.
No.
When I say my mother protected us, I mean my mother protected us.
I may say, incidentally, that this took on a shape in the end-- I'll tell you about that later when we come to Kristallnacht-- that my wife and I quite deliberately decided not to do that.
Well, again, you see, things had happened, you know, point by point.
And you know, what else can happen?
People would ask themselves.
You see, and many people would try to say, well, but nothing else can happen, you know?
Certainly, nobody considered the final solution.
There also was that.
You see, I suppose, just as anywhere else, the-- the people have to defend themselves against extremely unpleasant occurrences.
And I think some of us did it-- not us, I mean, adults-- did it by sort of denying it or saying, this can't get any worse, or things of that kind.
I'm not sure whether I'm answering your question.
Oh.
Well, again, there isn't all that much I can tell you.
You want to talk to my brother.
He was there.
By the time, I was already in Berlin, at this Berlin school.
I knew that something was going on.
I didn't dare call home.
And home didn't tell me for weeks.
Telephone calls, people moving out of their house, somewhere else.
A Nazi showing up at the apartment that I was at, my-- with my aunts and uncles.
And one uncle, who I think never was bigger than 5'3' in his life, faced a Nazi in full uniform.
He wanted the apartment.
And my uncle told him to go and [? drown ?] himself and bringing him documents or else no.
And the man left.
I think, I seem to remember, that my uncles, in the end, did not stay in the apartment.
Where they went, I do not know.
I knew next day, from the news and from the-- from the rumor mill, the-- you know, the grapevine, that many shops had been destroyed and many synagogues have been destroyed and so on, you see?
Now, I didn't know this at the time, but the-- my father was one of the very first men in Chemnitz to be taken.
And he was taken by the Chemnitz police, who put him into a prison cell.
And we are pretty sure, in our own minds, that they did this in order to save him, to take him out of the hands of the Nazi gangs.
Because three times during the day, the proper Nazi gangs, with their, you know, uniforms on, showed up and demanded him.
And went away when they found he wasn't there.
And I'm convinced he wouldn't have lived to the street corner if they had got him.
The police held him as long as they could, apparently.
And then he went to-- was sent to Buchenwald.
Well, that would be the early morning of the 10th, I suppose.
See, because we called Kristallnacht 9th through the 10th, you see?
Well, in the end, even my mother had to tell me that father was in a concentration camp, and he was in Buchenwald.
And I happened to know that she ran from pillar to post using every line she knew that, you know, had-- where my father had been-- had obligated them, to see whether she could get him out.
And we have a suspicion that she was successful.
The reason that we this suspicion is that the day he was released, after about three or four weeks, his name was not in alphabetical order, as most names were.
His name was at the end.
It sounds or looks a bit as though it had been some extra or afterthought, or something.
So we think she may have had some luck.
He came home at what turned out to be about exactly his 50th birthday.
But I was told that.
I mean, after a while, I mean, even my mother couldn't keep a radio away from me, you see.
So you were in Berlin while all this was happening.
Mm.
Mm.
Well, I may be-- I may be making this a bit too sharp.
They didn't sit down and discuss it with me.
But of course, I overheard-- I mean, they didn't try to keep it secret either.
I mean, I did know that there was some pretty startling things going on.
Not like my brother, you see?
My mother called a taxi to drive my brother and her somewhere.
And they went past the synagogue, which was in ruins and still burning.
And she passed a shop, a Jewish shop, belonging to a Jew.
It had been smashed.
And in the smashed window, lay a Torah.
I mean, he knew.
He was there.
I mean, he-- he knows Kristallnacht, you see, you might say, more sharply than I.
Oh yeah, yeah, sure.
All right.
All right.
I knew a number of things.
Number one, you know, I have forgotten by now whether this was before-- shortly before or shortly afterwards.
But I know that my father took over the presidency of this [INAUDIBLE] group.
And when I came home and congratulated him on it, he shh, shh, shh, shh.
Quiet. ?] He didn't want mummy to hear it.
Because mummy was very much against his still being prominent and you know, in position to do something.

I know now-- I don't know when I learned this-- that he was fired by the Nazi takeover chap from his position as director of the [INAUDIBLE] bank for not being on duty on the November the 10th, and the weeks after.
And my mother had a big job trying to persuade him not to try to sue the Nazi.
You know, my father was still in a position and able or wanting to-- to-- to fight these things.
I also know that my mother took it upon herself, and father then went along, to start emigration proceedings.
She went to the American embassy, of course.
And I have a document, if you like to see it, the letter that she has been-- you know, the family has been put on a waiting list and so on and so on.
We know today the waiting list would have been 10 years.
I forget how many.
We know that the United States did not accept one threatened Jew or other persecutee from Germany or Austria.
Not one, in the entire history.
Most people have heard about the Saint Louis.
I know somebody who was on it.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] She went to-- to-- other places.
And I have the passport of my mother and my father.
If you like you can see it later on.
In which he has got-- they have got-- permission to enter Panama, Haiti, Shanghai, England.
The English visa I was able to procure.
And it's a long and complicated story.
But I was able to procure it, even though I was only 16, from England.
It came too late, you see?
How did it affect us?
Well, there's one way, you see?
My mother then urging the emigration.
And the next thing, of course, you see, November 18th-- November 10th, a few weeks later, I was told that I had been chosen for the children's transports to go to England.
What I didn't know at the time was that the British were accepting Jews from Germany and Austria, if they could find someone to, so to speak, guarantee them, to have some sort of you know--
--intention of looking after them.
And about 100,000 Jews got into England.
And England was, at that time, preparing for war.
But the British government--
OK.
